title: MITRE ATT&CK - Initial Access
content: Initial Access describes adversary behavior used to gain an initial foothold in an environment. A successful unauthorized login to a remote service may indicate possible initial access, especially when preceded by suspicious authentication activity.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Execution
content: Execution describes adversary behavior that runs malicious code on a local or remote system. Suspicious PowerShell commands, encoded commands, unusual script execution, and unexpected process launches may indicate execution activity.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Persistence
content: Persistence describes adversary behavior that maintains access to systems across restarts, credential changes, or other interruptions. Unexpected account creation, scheduled tasks, services, or startup modifications may indicate persistence.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Defense Evasion
content: Defense Evasion describes adversary behavior used to avoid detection. Encoded commands, obfuscation, disabled logging, log clearing, and suspicious changes to security controls may indicate defense evasion.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Credential Access
content: Credential Access describes adversary behavior used to steal account names, passwords, tokens, or other credentials. Brute force activity, password spraying, suspicious authentication failures, and MFA fatigue may indicate credential access attempts.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Discovery
content: Discovery describes adversary behavior used to learn about a system or network after gaining access. Commands that enumerate users, groups, network configuration, processes, or domain information may indicate discovery activity.

title: MITRE ATT&CK - Command and Control
content: Command and Control describes adversary behavior used to communicate with systems under their control. Suspicious outbound connections, beaconing behavior, unknown domains, and repeated callbacks may indicate command and control activity.

title: SOC Checklist - Containment Principles
content: Containment recommendations should be proportional to the evidence. Common containment steps include disabling or resetting affected accounts, isolating impacted hosts, blocking confirmed malicious indicators, preserving evidence, and escalating for human approval before destructive or irreversible action.

title: SOC Checklist - Common Evidence Gaps
content: Common evidence gaps in SOC triage include missing endpoint telemetry, missing authentication history, missing VPN or MFA logs, missing process tree information, missing user confirmation, missing asset criticality, and missing timeline context.

title: SOC Checklist - Brute Force Followed by Successful Login
content: For brute force followed by successful login, review authentication history, confirm whether the login was expected, check MFA or VPN context if available, inspect post-login activity, review privilege changes, check for persistence, and consider disabling the account until legitimacy is verified.

title: SOC Checklist - Suspicious PowerShell
content: For suspicious PowerShell activity, review the full command line, parent process, user context, script block logs, network connections, file writes, encoded content, and whether the activity matches approved administrative behavior.
